name: How does flooring installation work on stairs, and is it more expensive?
headline: How does flooring installation work on stairs, and is it more expensive?

mainEntity.name: How does flooring installation work on stairs, and is it more expensive?
mainEntity.text: How does flooring installation work on stairs, and is it more expensive?

acceptedAnswer.text: Installing new flooring on stairs is more detailed and labor-intensive than on flat floors, so it often costs more per step. For hardwood, laminate, or vinyl on stairs, each tread (the part you step on) and riser (the vertical part) has to be carefully measured, cut, and fitted. You usually need matching stair noses or bullnose pieces for a safe, finished front edge. Carpet on stairs is a bit different but still requires careful stretching and fastening. Expect stair work to take extra time. A full staircase can sometimes take as long as a large room. If your stairs are uneven, squeaky, or out of square, the installer may need to do repairs before installing the new material. Safety is a big concern. The stair surface should not be too slippery, especially for kids, older adults, or pets. Some products have textured finishes or you can add stair treads or grip strips. Local code may also have rules about stair nosing and dimensions, which a good installer should know. A common mistake is assuming stairs are included in a “per square foot” price. Stair pricing is often separate. Be sure your estimate clearly lists how many steps are included, what materials will be used, and how the edges and side details will be handled.
